refashioned: We have previously said that one ought to choose the intermediate, not the excess or the deficiency, and that the intermediate is determined by what the right rule prescribes. Let us now examine the nature of this right rule. In all the states of character we have discussed, as in every other discipline, there is a target at which the person who possesses the rule aims, adjusting effort accordingly; and there is a standard that determines the mean states we call intermediate between excess and deficiency, those that accord with the right rule. But this statement, though true, is far from clear. For not only here, but in every pursuit that admits of systematic knowledge, it is true to say that we must exert ourselves neither too much nor too little but to an intermediate degree, as the right rule dictates. Yet if someone possessed only this knowledge, they would be no wiser for it. We would not know what medicines to apply to our bodies if someone merely said 'all those that the medical art prescribes, and that accord with the practice of a skilled physician.' Hence, regarding the states of the soul as well, it is necessary not only to make this true but general statement, but also to determine what the right rule is and what standard fixes it.
elegant: We have established that virtue lies in the mean as determined by the right rule, but saying 'follow the right rule' is no more helpful than telling a sick person to 'do whatever medicine prescribes.' We must specify what the right rule actually is and what standard determines it.
summary: Aristotle opens Book VI by noting that the doctrine of the mean requires a standard -- the 'right rule' -- to be practically useful. Simply telling someone to follow the right rule without defining it is as unhelpful as telling a patient to take whatever medicine prescribes. The task now is to determine what this right rule is.
distilled: The doctrine of the mean is incomplete without an account of what the 'right rule' actually is and how it is determined.
notes: This passage picks up directly from Book II's discussion of virtue as a mean (II.6, 1106b36-1107a2), where the mean is said to be determined by the 'right rule' (orthos logos). The medical analogy is characteristic of Aristotle's method. -- The question 'what is the right rule?' effectively motivates the entire investigation of Book VI, which will culminate in identifying practical wisdom (phronesis) as the faculty that determines it.

refashioned: We divided the virtues of the soul into virtues of character and virtues of intellect. Having discussed the moral virtues in detail, let us now address the intellectual virtues, beginning with some observations about the soul. We said before that the soul has two parts: one that grasps a rational principle and one that is irrational. Let us now make a similar division within the rational part itself. Assume there are two rational faculties: one by which we contemplate things whose fundamental causes are invariable, and one by which we contemplate things that are variable. For where objects differ in kind, the corresponding part of the soul differs in kind as well, since these faculties possess their knowledge by virtue of a certain likeness and kinship with their objects. Let the first of these be called the scientific faculty and the second the calculative faculty, since to deliberate and to calculate are the same thing, and no one deliberates about what cannot be otherwise. The calculative faculty, then, is one division of the rational part of the soul. We must therefore determine what the best state of each of these two faculties is, for that best state is the virtue of each.
elegant: The rational soul divides into two faculties: the scientific, which contemplates what is necessary and invariable, and the calculative, which contemplates what is variable and subject to deliberation. Each faculty has its own virtue -- its own optimal state -- and we must determine what these are.
summary: Aristotle divides the rational part of the soul into two faculties: one for necessary, invariable truths (the scientific faculty) and one for variable, contingent matters (the calculative faculty). The virtue of each faculty is its best possible state, which he sets out to identify.
distilled: The rational soul has two parts -- one for necessary truths, one for contingent matters -- and each has its own distinctive excellence.
notes: The twofold division of the rational soul corresponds to the division between theoretical and practical knowledge that runs throughout Aristotle's philosophy. -- The principle that the soul's faculties are differentiated by their objects (like is known by like) appears also in De Anima III.4-5. -- The scientific (epistemonikon) and calculative (logistikon) distinction grounds the later taxonomy of the five intellectual virtues (6.3ff). -- Books II-V covered the moral virtues; Book VI now turns to the intellectual virtues promised at I.13, 1103a3-10.

refashioned: The virtue of anything is relative to its proper function. Now there are three things in the soul that govern action and the attainment of truth: sensation, reason, and desire. Of these, sensation initiates no action; this is evident from the fact that animals possess sensation but have no share in deliberate action.
elegant: Three things in the soul govern action and truth: sensation, reason, and desire. Sensation alone produces no action, as shown by the fact that animals have sensation but do not act deliberately.
summary: Aristotle identifies sensation, reason, and desire as the three elements in the soul that control action and truth. He eliminates sensation as a source of action, since animals have it but do not perform deliberate actions.
distilled: Sensation, reason, and desire govern action and truth, but sensation alone never initiates action.
notes: The claim that animals lack deliberate action (praxis) while possessing sensation connects to Aristotle's account in De Anima III.9-11, where desire and cognition together produce movement. -- 'Action' (praxis) here means specifically rational, deliberate conduct, not mere animal movement, which Aristotle attributes to appetite and phantasia (imagination) in De Anima III.10.

refashioned: What affirmation and negation are in thought, pursuit and avoidance are in desire. Since moral virtue is a state of character concerned with choice, and choice is deliberate desire, it follows that both the reasoning must be true and the desire right if the choice is to be good; and what desire pursues must be exactly what reasoning affirms. This kind of intellect and truth is practical. For the contemplative intellect, which is neither practical nor productive, the good and bad states are simply truth and falsity, since arriving at truth is the function of every intellectual faculty. But for the part of the intellect that is both practical and intellectual, the good state is truth in agreement with right desire.
elegant: In thought, affirmation and negation correspond to pursuit and avoidance in desire. Good choice requires true reasoning aligned with right desire. The contemplative intellect aims simply at truth; the practical intellect aims at truth that agrees with correct desire.
summary: Aristotle draws a parallel between thought and desire: just as thought affirms or denies, desire pursues or avoids. Good choice requires that reasoning be true and desire be rightly directed, with both in alignment. While the contemplative intellect seeks truth for its own sake, practical intellect succeeds when its truth accords with right desire.
distilled: Good choice requires the alignment of true reasoning with right desire -- practical truth is truth that corresponds to what one rightly wants.
notes: The concept of 'practical truth' (aletheia praktike) is distinctive to this passage and has been extensively debated. Some commentators (e.g., Aquinas, Broadie) read it as truth about what to do; others emphasize the agreement between intellect and desire. -- The parallel between affirmation/negation and pursuit/avoidance echoes De Anima III.7, 431a8-14. -- The distinction between contemplative, practical, and productive intellect structures Book VI's entire classification.

refashioned: The origin of action -- its efficient cause, not its final cause -- is choice, and the origin of choice is desire combined with reasoning directed toward an end. This is why choice cannot exist without both reason and intellect and a moral state of character, for good action and its opposite require a combination of intellect and character. Intellect by itself moves nothing; only intellect that aims at a goal and is practical has motivating power. Practical intellect also governs the productive intellect, since everyone who makes something makes it for an end. But what is made is not an end in the unqualified sense -- it is only an end in a particular relation, the end of a particular productive process. Only what is done through action is an end without qualification, for good action is itself an end, and desire aims at this. Hence choice is either desire informed by reasoning or reasoning informed by desire, and such a composite origin of action is what a human being essentially is. It should be noted that nothing past is an object of choice: no one chooses to have sacked Troy, for no one deliberates about the past but only about what is future and capable of being otherwise. What is past cannot not have occurred; hence Agathon is right in saying --
elegant: Choice -- the efficient cause of action -- is desire combined with means-end reasoning, and requires both intellect and moral character. Intellect alone moves nothing; only practical intellect directed at a goal motivates action. Good action, unlike production, is its own end. Choice is thus reasoning desire or desiring reason, and this composite is the essence of human agency. The past is beyond choice, since deliberation concerns only what can still be otherwise.
summary: Choice, the source of action, is a fusion of desire and reasoning toward an end. It requires both intellect and moral character. Unlike making things, where the product is the goal, good action is an end in itself. Aristotle defines choice as either 'reasoning desire' or 'desiring reason,' marking it as distinctively human.
distilled: Choice is the union of desire and practical reasoning, and this fusion of thought and appetite is the distinctive origin of human action.
notes: Agathon was a 5th-century BCE Athenian tragic poet, host of the banquet in Plato's Symposium. The quotation concludes in 6.6. -- The claim that 'intellect by itself moves nothing' (nous autos ouden kinei) is a key thesis, echoed in De Anima III.9, 432b26-433a1. -- 'Choice is desiderative reason or ratiocinative desire' (orexis dianoetike or orektikos nous) has been central to debates about Aristotle's action theory. -- The distinction between making (poiesis) and doing (praxis) is fundamental to Book VI and recurs in the account of art (6.9) and practical wisdom (6.10-14).

refashioned:
-- 'For this alone is lacking even to God: to make undone things that have once been done.'

The function of both intellectual parts of the soul, then, is truth. Therefore the states that most fully enable each part to attain truth are the virtues of those two parts.
elegant: Even God cannot undo what has been done. Since truth is the function of both rational faculties, their virtues are the states that best attain truth.
summary: Concluding the quotation from Agathon -- that not even God can change the past -- Aristotle states that truth is the proper function of both intellectual faculties, and their virtues are the states that most reliably produce truth.
distilled: The virtue of each intellectual faculty is whatever state best enables it to grasp truth.
notes: The Agathon quotation (fragment 5, Nauck) was well known in antiquity and is also cited in Plato's Symposium 197e in a different context. -- The identification of truth as the 'work' (ergon) of both rational parts sets up the classification of the five truth-attaining states in 6.7. -- The ergon (function) argument here parallels the function argument for human happiness in Book I.7, 1097b24-1098a20.

refashioned: Let us begin afresh and discuss these states once more. Assume that the states by virtue of which the soul grasps truth through affirmation or denial are five in number: art, scientific knowledge, practical wisdom, philosophic wisdom, and intuitive reason. We exclude judgment and opinion from this list because in these we can be mistaken.
elegant: There are five states through which the soul reliably grasps truth: art, scientific knowledge, practical wisdom, philosophic wisdom, and intuitive reason. Judgment and opinion are excluded because they admit of error.
summary: Aristotle enumerates five intellectual states that reliably yield truth: art (techne), scientific knowledge (episteme), practical wisdom (phronesis), philosophic wisdom (sophia), and intuitive reason (nous). Opinion and judgment are excluded because they can be false.
distilled: The soul possesses truth through exactly five reliable states: art, scientific knowledge, practical wisdom, philosophic wisdom, and intuitive reason.
notes: This is the foundational taxonomy of Book VI. The five states (hexeis) are: techne (art/craft), episteme (scientific/demonstrative knowledge), phronesis (practical wisdom), sophia (philosophic/theoretical wisdom), and nous (intuitive reason/intellect). -- The exclusion of doxa (opinion) and hypolepsis (judgment/supposition) because they admit of falsehood distinguishes these five as truth-guaranteeing states. -- This classification influenced the medieval and scholastic tradition extensively, especially Aquinas's commentary on the Ethics.

refashioned:
What scientific knowledge is, if we are to speak precisely and not rely on loose similarities, is clear from the following. We all assume that what we know scientifically cannot be otherwise; things that can be otherwise pass beyond our knowledge once they leave our observation, and we cannot tell whether they still exist. Therefore the object of scientific knowledge exists of necessity, and what exists of necessity is eternal -- ungenerated and imperishable.

Furthermore, every science is thought to be teachable, and its object learnable. All teaching proceeds from what is already known, as we maintain in the Analytics: sometimes through induction and sometimes through deductive reasoning. Induction provides the starting point that grounds knowledge of universals, while deductive reasoning proceeds from universals. There are therefore starting points from which deduction proceeds that are not themselves reached by deduction; they are acquired through induction.

Scientific knowledge, then, is a state enabling demonstration, with the additional defining characteristics we specify in the Analytics. A person has scientific knowledge when they believe in a certain way and the starting points are known to them, since if the starting points are not better known to them than the conclusion, they will possess knowledge only incidentally.

Let this serve as our account of scientific knowledge.
elegant: Scientific knowledge concerns what is necessary, eternal, and cannot be otherwise. It is a demonstrative capacity proceeding from first principles -- principles that are themselves grasped not through demonstration but through induction. One truly knows something scientifically only when the first principles are better known than the conclusion.
summary: Scientific knowledge deals exclusively with what is necessary and eternal. It is the capacity for demonstration from first principles, which must be grasped through induction rather than deduction. Genuine scientific knowledge requires that the knower understand the first principles more securely than the conclusions drawn from them.
distilled: Scientific knowledge is the capacity for demonstrative reasoning from necessary first principles that are themselves more certain than anything derived from them.
notes: The references to 'the Analytics' are to Aristotle's Posterior Analytics, especially I.1-3 on the nature of demonstration and the requirement that premises be 'better known' (gnorimotera) than the conclusion. -- The claim that the objects of episteme are necessary and eternal reflects the strict sense of scientific knowledge in Aristotle, contrasting with modern usage. -- Posterior Analytics I.2, 71b9-72a5 gives the fuller account of what demonstration requires. -- The distinction between induction (epagoge) and syllogism (syllogismos) as the two paths to knowledge is also discussed in Topics I.12.

refashioned:
Among variable things, we must distinguish between things made and things done. Making and acting are different (for the nature of this distinction we may rely even on discussions outside our school). Therefore, the reasoned state of capacity to act is different from the reasoned state of capacity to make; neither includes the other, for acting is not making, nor is making acting.

Now since architecture is an art, and is essentially a reasoned state of capacity to make, and since there is no art that is not such a state, nor any such state that is not an art, art is identical with a state of capacity to make involving a true course of reasoning. All art is concerned with bringing something into being -- that is, with contriving and figuring out how something may come into existence that is capable of either existing or not existing, and whose origin lies in the maker rather than in the thing made. For art deals neither with things that exist or come into being by necessity, nor with things that do so by nature, since these have their origin in themselves.

Since making and acting are different, art must belong to the domain of making, not acting. In a sense, chance and art deal with the same objects, as Agathon says: 'Art loves chance and chance loves art.'

Art, then, as stated, is a state concerned with making, involving a true course of reasoning; and lack of art, conversely, is a state concerned with making involving a false course of reasoning. Both deal with what is variable.
elegant: Making and acting are fundamentally different. Art is a reasoned capacity to make -- to bring into being things whose origin lies in the maker, not in nature or necessity. It involves true reasoning about how to produce something that could either exist or not exist. As Agathon noted, art and chance operate in the same domain of contingency.
summary: Art (techne) is defined as a reasoned state of capacity to make things, involving true reasoning about production. It deals with what is contingent -- things that could be otherwise -- and whose cause lies in the maker rather than in nature or necessity. Aristotle sharply distinguishes making from acting, placing art squarely in the domain of making.
distilled: Art is the capacity for making guided by true reasoning, operating in the domain of what is contingent and whose origin lies in the maker.
notes: The distinction between making (poiesis) and acting (praxis) is central to Book VI and ultimately to Aristotle's classification of the sciences into theoretical, practical, and productive (Metaphysics VI.1, 1025b25). -- 'Discussions outside our school' likely refers to works in general circulation (exoterikoi logoi), possibly including published Platonic dialogues. -- Agathon (c. 448-400 BCE), the tragic poet, is quoted again (see 6.5-6.6). The saying about art and chance captures how both operate in the domain of contingency. -- The claim that art's origin is in the maker, not the thing made, contrasts with nature (physis), where the origin of change is in the thing itself (Physics II.1, 192b13-23).

refashioned: To understand practical wisdom, let us consider who the people are that we credit with possessing it. It is thought to be the mark of the practically wise person to be able to deliberate well about what is good and beneficial for themselves -- not in some particular respect, such as what conduces to health or to strength, but about what conduces to the good life in general. This is shown by the fact that we credit people with practical wisdom in a particular respect when they have calculated well toward some good end that falls outside the scope of any art.
elegant: The practically wise person deliberates well not about some particular good like health or strength, but about what promotes the good life as a whole. We attribute practical wisdom when someone calculates well toward good ends that no specific art covers.
summary: Practical wisdom is identified by looking at whom we consider practically wise: those who deliberate well about what is good for life as a whole, not merely about particular goods like health or strength. It applies where no specialized art provides the answer.
distilled: Practical wisdom is the ability to deliberate well about what conduces to the good life as a whole, not just to any particular good.
notes: Aristotle's method of beginning with endoxa (reputable opinions) and phainomena (what appears to be the case) is characteristic of his ethical methodology, described in VII.1, 1145b2-7. -- The distinction between practical wisdom and particular arts connects to Book I's discussion of the 'architectonic' good that encompasses all particular goods (I.2, 1094a26-b7). -- The phrase 'good life in general' (eu zen holos) links practical wisdom back to eudaimonia (happiness/flourishing) as defined in Book I.

refashioned: It follows that in the general sense, the person who is capable of deliberating well possesses practical wisdom. Now, no one deliberates about what is invariable or about what is impossible for them to do. Therefore, since scientific knowledge involves demonstration and there can be no demonstration of things whose first principles are variable (since all such things could actually be otherwise), and since it is impossible to deliberate about what is necessary, practical wisdom cannot be either scientific knowledge or art. It is not scientific knowledge because what can be done is capable of being otherwise; it is not art because action and making are different kinds of thing. The remaining alternative, then, is that practical wisdom is a true and reasoned state of capacity to act with regard to things that are good or bad for human beings.
elegant: Practical wisdom cannot be scientific knowledge, since what can be done is always capable of being otherwise; nor can it be art, since action and making are fundamentally different. It must therefore be a true, reasoned capacity to act concerning what is good or bad for human beings.
summary: Aristotle eliminates scientific knowledge and art as candidates for practical wisdom through a process of exclusion. Scientific knowledge deals with the necessary, but action concerns the variable. Art deals with making, but practical wisdom deals with acting. Practical wisdom is therefore defined as a true and reasoned capacity to act regarding human goods.
distilled: Practical wisdom is a true, reasoned capacity to act well regarding what is good and bad for human beings -- distinct from both science and art.
notes: The definition reached here (hexis alethes meta logou praktike peri ta anthropo agatha kai kaka) is one of the most important in the Ethics and has generated extensive commentary. -- The method of elimination (it is not X, not Y, so it must be Z) mirrors the procedure used in Posterior Analytics II for arriving at definitions. -- The distinction between practical wisdom (concerned with praxis) and art (concerned with poiesis) was introduced in 6.9 and is here applied to complete the definition.

refashioned:
For while making always has an end beyond itself, action cannot, since good action is itself its end. It is for this reason that we think Pericles and people like him possess practical wisdom: because they can see what is good for themselves and what is good for people in general. We consider that those who can manage households or states well have this capacity.

This is also why we call temperance 'sophrosune' -- the name implies that it preserves one's practical wisdom ('sozousa ten phronesin'). And what it preserves is a judgment of the kind we have described.
elegant: Good action, unlike production, is its own end. This is why we attribute practical wisdom to statesmen like Pericles who can discern what is good both for themselves and for others. Temperance preserves practical wisdom -- hence the Greek name sophrosune, suggesting 'that which saves phronesis.'
summary: Good action is an end in itself, unlike making. Pericles exemplifies practical wisdom because he could discern what is good for individuals and for the community. Aristotle offers an etymology of temperance (sophrosune) as 'that which preserves practical wisdom,' suggesting the two virtues are deeply connected.
distilled: Good action is its own end, and practical wisdom -- the ability to see what is truly good for oneself and others -- is preserved by temperance.
notes: Pericles (c. 495-429 BCE) was the leading Athenian statesman during the golden age of Athens, celebrated for his political judgment and rhetorical skill. Thucydides portrays him as a model of deliberative excellence. -- The etymology of sophrosune as 'sozousa ten phronesin' (saving/preserving practical wisdom) is a Platonic-Aristotelian wordplay; Plato gives a similar etymology in the Cratylus 411e. -- The claim that praxis is its own end while poiesis has an external end is fundamental to Aristotle's practical philosophy and distinguishes his ethics from purely consequentialist frameworks.

refashioned:
For it is not just any judgment that pleasant and painful experiences destroy and distort -- not, for example, the judgment that a triangle does or does not have angles equal to two right angles -- but only judgments about what is to be done. For the originating causes of action consist in the end at which actions aim; but the person who has been corrupted by pleasure or pain immediately fails to see any such originating cause -- fails to see that for the sake of this end, or because of this end, they ought to choose and do whatever they choose and do. For vice destroys the originating cause of action.

Practical wisdom, then, must be a reasoned and true state of capacity to act with regard to human goods.
elegant: Pleasure and pain do not corrupt mathematical judgments but do corrupt practical ones -- they blind us to the ends for which we should act. Vice destroys our grasp of action's originating causes. Thus practical wisdom must be a reasoned, true capacity to act regarding human goods.
summary: Pleasure and pain selectively corrupt practical judgment, not theoretical knowledge like geometry. When someone is ruined by pleasure or pain, they can no longer perceive the correct ends of action. Since vice destroys our grasp of these ends, practical wisdom requires both true reasoning and right orientation toward human goods.
distilled: Vice corrupts not our grasp of abstract truths but our perception of the ends worth pursuing, which is why practical wisdom requires moral soundness.
notes: The claim that pleasure and pain distort practical but not theoretical judgment is crucial to Aristotle's view that moral virtue and practical wisdom are interdependent. -- This anticipates the stronger claim in 6.35 that one cannot be practically wise without being good. -- The idea that vice 'destroys the originating cause' (arche) of action connects to Book VII's discussion of incontinence (akrasia), where the incontinent person's judgment is temporarily overridden by desire. -- The triangle example illustrates the immunity of mathematical truths to motivational corruption.

refashioned:
Furthermore, while there is such a thing as excellence in art, there is no such thing as excellence in practical wisdom. In art, the person who errs willingly is preferable to one who errs unwillingly, but in practical wisdom -- as in the moral virtues -- it is the reverse. Plainly, then, practical wisdom is a virtue and not an art.

Since there are two parts of the soul that can follow a course of reasoning, practical wisdom must be the virtue of one of them -- namely, the part that forms opinions. For opinion deals with what is variable, and so does practical wisdom. Yet practical wisdom is not merely a reasoned state, as shown by the fact that such a state can be forgotten, but practical wisdom cannot.
elegant: Unlike art, where deliberate error shows greater skill, in practical wisdom deliberate error is worse -- making practical wisdom a genuine virtue, not merely a skill. It belongs to the soul's opinion-forming faculty, which deals with variable things. But it exceeds a mere reasoned state, since it cannot be forgotten as ordinary knowledge can.
summary: Aristotle distinguishes practical wisdom from art by noting that in art, someone who errs on purpose shows greater mastery, while in practical wisdom the reverse holds -- deliberate moral error is worse. This proves practical wisdom is a virtue. It belongs to the calculative faculty that deals with variable things, but goes beyond mere rational capacity because, unlike ordinary knowledge, it cannot be forgotten.
distilled: Practical wisdom is a virtue rather than an art, because deliberate error in it is a deficiency, not a sign of mastery, and unlike mere knowledge it cannot be forgotten.
notes: The contrast between voluntary error in art versus virtue comes from Plato's Hippias Minor (375d-376c), where Socrates provocatively argues that the person who errs willingly is better. Aristotle here tacitly corrects Plato by restricting this to techne. -- The claim that practical wisdom 'cannot be forgotten' suggests it is more like a character trait than a body of information -- it is a settled disposition (hexis) that integrates with one's entire way of being. -- Practical wisdom is assigned to the calculative (doxastikon) part of the rational soul, as established in 6.2.

refashioned:
Scientific knowledge is judgment about things that are universal and necessary, and the conclusions of demonstration -- all scientific knowledge -- follow from first principles, since scientific knowledge involves apprehension of a rational ground. This being so, the first principle from which what is scientifically known follows cannot itself be an object of scientific knowledge, art, or practical wisdom. It cannot be scientifically known because what is scientifically known can be demonstrated, and first principles are not reached by demonstration. It cannot be grasped by art or practical wisdom because these deal with things that are variable.

Nor are first principles the objects of philosophic wisdom, for it is characteristic of the philosopher to possess demonstrations about certain things. If, then, the states of mind by which we grasp truth and are never deceived about things invariable or variable are scientific knowledge, practical wisdom, philosophic wisdom, and intuitive reason -- and if it cannot be any of the first three -- then the remaining alternative is that it is intuitive reason that grasps the first principles.
elegant: First principles cannot be grasped through demonstration (since they ground demonstration), nor through art or practical wisdom (which concern variable things), nor through philosophic wisdom (which includes demonstration). By elimination, first principles must be grasped by intuitive reason.
summary: Aristotle argues by elimination that first principles -- the foundations of all demonstration -- must be grasped by intuitive reason (nous). They cannot be objects of scientific knowledge (which requires demonstration from them), art or practical wisdom (which deal with variable things), or philosophic wisdom (which itself involves demonstration).
distilled: First principles, which ground all demonstration but cannot themselves be demonstrated, are grasped by intuitive reason alone.
notes: This argument parallels Posterior Analytics II.19, where Aristotle explains how nous grasps the indemonstrable starting points of science through a process beginning with perception. -- The problem of first principles (how we know them if not by demonstration) was recognized by Plato as leading to a 'regress' problem (Republic 510b-511d). Aristotle's solution through nous avoids infinite regress. -- The elimination argument (it must be one of the five; it cannot be these four; therefore it is the fifth) is a characteristic Aristotelian dialectical procedure.

refashioned: In the arts, we ascribe wisdom to the most accomplished practitioners -- to Phidias as a sculptor and to Polyclitus as a maker of statues -- and by 'wisdom' here we mean nothing more than excellence in art. But we also think some people are wise in general, not merely in some particular field or other limited respect, as Homer says in the Margites:
elegant: In the arts, 'wisdom' simply means supreme skill -- as we attribute it to Phidias in sculpture or Polyclitus in statue-making. But we also speak of wisdom in a general, unrestricted sense, as Homer notes in the Margites.
summary: Aristotle distinguishes two senses of wisdom: in the narrow sense, it means supreme excellence within a particular art (as with Phidias in sculpture); in the broader sense, it means a general wisdom not limited to any particular domain.
distilled: Wisdom in its full sense is not mastery of any particular art but a comprehensive excellence of understanding.
notes: Phidias (c. 480-430 BCE) was the most celebrated sculptor of ancient Greece, responsible for the Parthenon sculptures and the great statue of Zeus at Olympia. Polyclitus (5th century BCE) was famous for his bronze statues and his Canon, a treatise on ideal proportions. -- The Margites was a comic epic attributed to Homer in antiquity, now largely lost. Aristotle cites it also in Poetics 4, 1448b30. -- The two senses of sophia (craft-wisdom vs. general wisdom) set up Aristotle's formal definition of philosophic wisdom in 6.17.

refashioned:
'Him did the gods make neither a digger nor yet a ploughman, nor wise in anything else.'

Therefore wisdom must plainly be the most complete and finished form of knowledge. It follows that the wise person must not only know what follows from first principles, but must also possess truth about the first principles themselves. Therefore philosophic wisdom must be intuitive reason combined with scientific knowledge -- scientific knowledge of the highest objects, which has received, as it were, its proper completion.
elegant: Wisdom is the most complete form of knowledge. The wise person must both know what follows from first principles and possess truth about those principles themselves. Philosophic wisdom is therefore intuitive reason combined with scientific knowledge of the highest things.
summary: Completing the Homer quotation, Aristotle defines philosophic wisdom (sophia) as the most finished form of knowledge. It combines intuitive reason, which grasps first principles, with scientific knowledge, which derives conclusions from them -- and it is directed at the highest objects of understanding.
distilled: Philosophic wisdom is intuitive reason united with scientific knowledge, directed at the highest objects of understanding.
notes: The definition of sophia as nous + episteme directed at the highest objects is Aristotle's formal account. 'Highest objects' (ta timiotata) will be specified in 6.18-19 as the most divine things in the cosmos. -- This definition makes sophia a compound virtue, incorporating both the capacity to grasp first principles (nous) and the capacity to demonstrate from them (episteme). -- The formulation 'scientific knowledge that has received its proper completion' (hoion kephalen echousa) literally means 'having received its head/capstone,' a vivid architectural metaphor.

refashioned:
We say 'of the highest objects' because it would be strange to think that political science or practical wisdom is the highest form of knowledge, since human beings are not the highest thing in the universe.

Now if what is healthy or good differs for humans and for fishes, but what is white or straight is always the same, everyone would say that what is wise is always the same but what is practically wise differs. For it is to whatever creature observes well the various matters concerning its own life that one ascribes practical wisdom, and it is to this creature that one would entrust such matters. This is why we say that even some of the lower animals possess practical wisdom -- those that are found to have a capacity for foresight regarding their own lives.

It is also evident that philosophic wisdom and political science cannot be the same. For if the state of mind concerned with one's own interests were to be called philosophic wisdom, there would be many philosophic wisdoms -- not one concerned with the good of all animals, any more than there is one art of medicine for all existing things, but a different philosophic wisdom for each species.
elegant: It would be absurd to call practical wisdom the highest knowledge, since humans are not the highest beings. What counts as wise is universal, but what counts as practically wise varies by species -- even some animals display practical foresight regarding their own lives. Philosophic wisdom therefore cannot be the same as political science; otherwise there would be as many 'wisdoms' as there are species.
summary: Philosophic wisdom cannot be identified with practical wisdom or political science, because humans are not the most important beings in the universe. While practical wisdom varies by species -- even some animals display it as foresight about their own survival -- philosophic wisdom concerns universal, unchanging truths that transcend any species' particular interests.
distilled: Philosophic wisdom transcends species-relative concerns, since what is wise is universal, while what is practically wise varies for each kind of being.
notes: The claim that 'man is not the best thing in the world' reflects Aristotle's cosmological hierarchy, in which the celestial bodies and unmoved mover surpass human beings in dignity. -- Attributing practical wisdom to animals (e.g., cranes, bees) appears to be a deliberate departure from the strictest definition, using 'practical wisdom' in an extended sense for prudential foresight. -- The argument against identifying sophia with political wisdom anticipates 6.40's claim that practical wisdom serves philosophic wisdom rather than ruling over it.

refashioned:
And if one argues that human beings are the highest of the animals, that makes no difference, for there are other things far more divine in their nature than humans -- most conspicuously, the celestial bodies of which the heavens are composed.

From what has been said, it is plain that philosophic wisdom is scientific knowledge combined with intuitive reason, directed at the things that are highest by nature. This is why we say that Anaxagoras, Thales, and people like them possess philosophic but not practical wisdom, when we see them ignorant of what is to their own advantage. We say that they know things that are remarkable, admirable, difficult, and divine, but useless -- because it is not human goods that they seek.
elegant: Even if humans are the highest animals, there exist far more divine things -- above all, the celestial bodies. Philosophic wisdom is therefore scientific knowledge combined with intuitive reason, concerning what is highest by nature. Thinkers like Anaxagoras and Thales possess this wisdom while lacking practical wisdom, knowing things remarkable and divine but useless for everyday life.
summary: Aristotle completes his argument that philosophic wisdom concerns things higher than human affairs, pointing to the celestial bodies. He illustrates the distinction by noting that Anaxagoras and Thales possessed philosophic wisdom -- understanding remarkable and divine matters -- while being famously impractical about their own worldly interests.
distilled: Philosophic wisdom grasps what is highest in nature -- remarkable, divine, but indifferent to human advantage -- as exemplified by Anaxagoras and Thales.
notes: Anaxagoras (c. 500-428 BCE) was a pre-Socratic philosopher who proposed nous (mind) as the organizing principle of the cosmos; he was prosecuted in Athens for impiety. Thales (c. 624-546 BCE) was traditionally the first Greek philosopher, known for predicting a solar eclipse and for the thesis that everything is water. Both were proverbially impractical. -- The story of Thales falling into a well while stargazing (Plato, Theaetetus 174a) illustrates this contrast. Aristotle tells a counter-story of Thales' olive-press speculation in Politics I.11, 1259a5-18. -- The 'bodies of which the heavens are framed' refers to the celestial spheres, composed of aether (the fifth element) in Aristotle's cosmology (De Caelo I.2-3).

refashioned:
Practical wisdom, on the other hand, is concerned with things human and things about which it is possible to deliberate. For we say this is above all the work of the practically wise person: to deliberate well. But no one deliberates about things that are invariable, nor about things that lack an achievable good end that can be brought about by action. The person who is unconditionally good at deliberating is the one capable of aiming, through calculation, at the best for human beings among things attainable by action.

Nor is practical wisdom concerned with universals only -- it must also recognize particulars, for it is practical, and practice concerns particulars. This is why some who lack theoretical knowledge, especially those with experience, are more effective in practice than others who have such knowledge. For if someone knew that light meats are digestible and wholesome but did not know which sorts of meat are light, they would not produce health; but the person who knows that chicken is wholesome is more likely to produce health.

Since practical wisdom is concerned with action, one should possess both universal and particular knowledge, or, if only one, the particular in preference to the universal. But as with philosophic wisdom, so with practical wisdom there must be a controlling, architectonic form.
elegant: Practical wisdom concerns human affairs and what can be achieved through deliberation and action. It requires not just universal principles but knowledge of particulars -- someone who knows 'light meats are healthy' but not that chicken is light will fail in practice. Experience with particulars often outweighs abstract knowledge. One needs both forms of knowledge, but if forced to choose, particular knowledge is more useful for action.
summary: Practical wisdom deals with human affairs and requires both universal principles and particular knowledge. Someone who knows general rules but lacks particular knowledge will fail in practice, which is why experienced people often outperform those with only theoretical understanding. If one must choose, particular knowledge is more practically useful, though ideally one should have both.
distilled: Practical wisdom requires knowledge of both universals and particulars, but knowledge of the particular is more important for action than knowledge of the universal alone.
notes: The chicken example is characteristically Aristotelian -- concrete, mundane, and effective. It illustrates the primacy of particular knowledge for practical purposes. -- The claim that experience (empeiria) sometimes surpasses theoretical knowledge echoes Metaphysics I.1, 981a12-24, where Aristotle says experienced people are often more successful in action than those who know the universal but lack experience. -- The mention of an 'architectonic' form of practical wisdom sets up the discussion in 6.21 of political wisdom as the master form of phronesis. -- The emphasis on particulars anticipates 6.25's comparison of practical wisdom to perception.

refashioned: Political wisdom and practical wisdom are the same state of mind, though their essential nature differs. Among the forms of wisdom concerned with the city, the controlling, architectonic form is legislative wisdom, while the form related to legislation as particulars are related to universals is known by the general name 'political wisdom.' This latter form has to do with action and deliberation, for a decree is something to be carried out as an individual act. This is why those who engage in this work are alone said to 'take part in politics,' for they alone 'do things' in the way that manual laborers 'do things.'
elegant: Political wisdom and practical wisdom are the same capacity applied differently. Legislative wisdom is its architectonic form, setting universal frameworks; political wisdom in the narrower sense applies these to particular decisions and decrees. Only those who deal in such particulars are truly said to 'do politics.'
summary: Political wisdom and practical wisdom are the same mental capacity, differing only in application. Legislative wisdom is the universal, architectonic form that establishes broad frameworks, while political wisdom in the everyday sense deals with particular decisions and decrees -- the actual 'doing' of politics.
distilled: Political and practical wisdom are the same capacity; legislation provides the universal framework, while day-to-day politics applies it to particular actions.
notes: The identification of phronesis with politike in some sense connects to Book I.2, where politics is called the 'architectonic' science that orders all others. -- The observation that only those who deal in particular decrees are said to 'do politics' reflects Aristotle's awareness that 'politics' in common usage meant the active, particular work of governing, not abstract theorizing about constitutions. -- The analogy with manual laborers (cheirotechnai) suggests that both politicians and craftsmen are valued for their practical engagement with particulars.

refashioned:
Practical wisdom is also identified especially with the form of it that concerns oneself as an individual, and this is what commonly bears the general name 'practical wisdom.' The other forms are called household management, legislation, and politics -- the last being divided into deliberative and judicial branches.

Now knowing what is good for oneself is one kind of knowledge, but it differs greatly from the other kinds. The person who knows and attends to their own interests is thought to have practical wisdom, while politicians are considered busybodies. Hence the words of Euripides:
elegant: Practical wisdom in its most personal form -- knowing what is good for oneself -- is what people typically mean by the term. Other branches include household management, legislation, and politics (deliberative and judicial). People who attend to their own interests are called practically wise, while politicians are dismissed as busybodies, as Euripides suggests.
summary: In common usage, practical wisdom primarily refers to the individual capacity to know what is good for oneself. Its broader forms include household management, legislation, and politics. There is a popular tendency to view self-interested prudence as true wisdom and political engagement as meddlesome busywork.
distilled: People most readily call 'practically wise' those who manage their own affairs well, often dismissing political engagement as mere busybodiness.
notes: Euripides (c. 480-406 BCE) was one of the three great Athenian tragedians. The quotation continues in 6.23. -- The division into household management (oikonomia), legislation (nomothetike), and politics (politike) maps onto the three practical sciences Aristotle recognizes. -- The popular identification of practical wisdom with self-interested prudence is a view Aristotle will partially correct: genuine practical wisdom, he argues, necessarily involves concern for others, since the individual good cannot be separated from the common good.

refashioned:
'But how could I be wise, who might at ease, numbered among the army's multitude, have had an equal share? ... For those who aim too high and do too much ...'

Those who think this way seek their own good and consider that one ought to do so. From this opinion, then, has come the view that such people have practical wisdom. Yet perhaps one's own good cannot exist without household management or a form of government. Furthermore, how one should order one's own affairs is not at all obvious and requires inquiry.
elegant: The Euripides quotation voices the view that wisdom means quietly looking after oneself rather than overreaching in public life. But this view is questionable: one's own good may be inseparable from household management and political order. How to manage one's own affairs is itself far from obvious.
summary: Aristotle cites Euripides to illustrate the popular view that wisdom consists in quietly minding one's own business. He challenges this, noting that personal good may require sound household management and political order, and that managing one's own life is itself a difficult problem requiring inquiry.
distilled: One's own good cannot be fully separated from the good management of households and cities, and even self-governance requires serious deliberation.
notes: The Euripides quotation is from the Philoctetes (fragment 787 Nauck), a lost play. The sentiment reflects a quietist stance common in some Greek thought, contrasting with the Periclean ideal of active citizenship. -- Aristotle's response -- that individual good requires political order -- is consistent with his claim in Politics I.2 that humans are by nature political animals (zoon politikon, 1253a2-3). -- The observation that ordering one's own affairs 'is not clear and needs inquiry' is a characteristically modest Aristotelian disclaimer that motivates further investigation.

refashioned:
What has been said is confirmed by the fact that while young people can become geometricians and mathematicians and wise in such subjects, a young person of practical wisdom is not thought to exist. The reason is that practical wisdom is concerned not only with universals but with particulars, which become familiar only through experience, and a young person lacks experience -- for it is length of time that gives experience.

Indeed, one might also ask why a young person can become a mathematician but not a philosopher or a natural scientist. Is it not because the objects of mathematics exist by abstraction, while the first principles of philosophy and natural science come from experience? Young people lack genuine conviction about the latter, merely using the proper language, whereas the essence of mathematical objects is plain enough to them.
elegant: Young people can master mathematics but not practical wisdom, because practical wisdom requires experience with particulars, which only time provides. Mathematics deals in abstractions accessible to any capable mind, but philosophy and natural science rest on first principles derived from experience, about which the young have no real conviction despite using the right vocabulary.
summary: Aristotle explains why young people can excel at mathematics but lack practical wisdom: mathematics deals with abstractions, while practical wisdom requires experience with particular situations that only accumulates over time. Similarly, philosophy and natural science rest on experiential first principles that the young merely parrot without genuine understanding.
distilled: Practical wisdom, unlike mathematics, requires lived experience with particulars that only time can provide, which is why the young cannot possess it.
notes: This is one of Aristotle's most frequently cited passages on the relationship between age, experience, and wisdom. -- The distinction between mathematical objects (existing 'by abstraction,' ex aphareseos) and physical/ethical first principles (from experience) reflects Aristotle's philosophy of mathematics in Metaphysics XIII-XIV. -- The observation that young people 'merely use the proper language' without conviction anticipates modern discussions of surface-level versus deep understanding. -- 'Philosopher' here likely means 'natural philosopher' (physikos), not philosopher in the modern sense.

refashioned:
Furthermore, error in deliberation may concern either the universal or the particular: one may fail to know that all heavy water is unhealthy, or that this particular water is heavy.

That practical wisdom is not scientific knowledge is evident, for as has been said, it is concerned with the ultimate particular fact, since the thing to be done is of this nature. It is thus opposed to intuitive reason in a certain way: intuitive reason grasps the limiting first principles for which no further reason can be given, while practical wisdom grasps the ultimate particular, which is the object not of scientific knowledge but of perception -- not the perception of qualities belonging to a single sense, but a perception akin to that by which we perceive that the particular figure before us is a triangle. For in that direction too -- the direction of the ultimate particular, just as in the direction of the first principles -- there is a limit beyond which reasoning cannot go. But this is perception rather than practical wisdom, though it is a different kind of perception from that of the individual senses.
elegant: Practical wisdom grasps the ultimate particular -- the concrete situation demanding action -- just as intuitive reason grasps first principles at the other extreme. This grasp of particulars resembles perception more than scientific knowledge, though it is not ordinary sense-perception but something like the immediate recognition that a figure is a triangle.
summary: Practical wisdom deals with ultimate particulars -- the concrete facts of the situation at hand -- just as intuitive reason deals with ultimate first principles. This grasp of particulars is more like perception than scientific knowledge, though it is a refined, intellectual form of perception rather than ordinary sense experience.
distilled: Practical wisdom grasps the concrete particular situation through a kind of intellectual perception, just as intuitive reason grasps the indemonstrable first principles.
notes: The comparison of practical wisdom's grasp of particulars to perception (aisthesis) is significant: it suggests that knowing what to do in a particular situation is not a matter of applying rules but of seeing what the situation requires. -- The triangle example illustrates non-sensory 'perception' -- recognizing that a shape is a triangle is an intellectual act, not a raw sensation. Cf. De Anima III.1, 425a14-b4 on the 'common sense.' -- The structural parallel between nous at the top (grasping first principles) and phronesis at the bottom (grasping particulars) creates a symmetry that Aristotle develops further in 6.30.

refashioned:
There is a difference between inquiry and deliberation, for deliberation is inquiry into a particular kind of thing. We must determine the nature of excellence in deliberation -- whether it is a form of scientific knowledge, opinion, skill in conjecture, or something else entirely.

It is not scientific knowledge, since people do not inquire about what they already know, but good deliberation is a kind of deliberation, and the person who deliberates inquires and calculates. Nor is it skill in conjecture, since conjecture involves no reasoning and is quick in operation, while deliberation takes time -- as the saying goes, one should carry out the conclusions of deliberation quickly, but deliberate slowly. Again, readiness of mind differs from excellence in deliberation; readiness of mind is a kind of skill in conjecture.

Nor is excellence in deliberation any kind of opinion. But since the person who deliberates badly makes a mistake while the person who deliberates well does so correctly, excellence in deliberation is clearly a kind of correctness -- though neither correctness of knowledge (since there is no error in knowledge) nor correctness of opinion (since correctness of opinion is simply truth, and everything that is an object of opinion is already determined).

Yet excellence in deliberation does involve reasoning. The remaining alternative, then, is that it is correctness of thinking. For thinking is not yet assertion -- while opinion has passed the inquiry stage and reached assertion, the person who deliberates, well or badly, is still searching and calculating.
elegant: Excellence in deliberation (euboulia) is not scientific knowledge, conjecture, or opinion. It is a form of correct thinking -- the reasoning process of inquiry and calculation that has not yet reached the stage of assertion. One should deliberate slowly but execute quickly.
summary: Aristotle defines excellence in deliberation (euboulia) by eliminating alternatives. It is not scientific knowledge (which precludes inquiry), not skill in conjecture (which is quick and unreasoned), and not opinion (which is already settled). It is correctness of thinking -- the accuracy of the reasoning process itself while one is still searching and calculating.
distilled: Excellence in deliberation is correctness in the process of thinking itself -- the accuracy of inquiry and calculation before one reaches a settled conclusion.
notes: Euboulia (excellence in deliberation, or 'good counsel') is introduced here as a subordinate capacity related to phronesis. -- The distinction between deliberating slowly and executing quickly became a common maxim in ancient practical wisdom literature. -- The careful elimination of alternatives (not episteme, not eustochia, not doxa) follows the same dialectical method used to define other intellectual virtues. -- The key insight is that euboulia concerns the process of reasoning, not the final product -- it is a procedural rather than a propositional excellence.

refashioned:
But excellence in deliberation is a certain correctness of deliberation, so we must first determine what deliberation is and what it is about. Since there is more than one kind of correctness, excellence in deliberation is clearly not just any kind. Consider: (1) the person lacking self-control and the bad person, if clever, will reach through calculation whatever goal they set for themselves, and so will have 'deliberated correctly' in a sense -- yet will have obtained a great evil. But having deliberated well is thought to be something good. Therefore, it is this kind of correctness in deliberation -- the kind that tends to attain what is genuinely good -- that constitutes excellence in deliberation.

(2) It is also possible to arrive at something good through a false chain of reasoning -- reaching the right conclusion but not by the right means, when the middle term is false. So this, too, is not yet excellence in deliberation: reaching the right goal but not by correct reasoning.

(3) Further, it is possible for one person to reach a good result through long deliberation while another reaches it quickly. Therefore, the slow result is not yet full excellence in deliberation either; rather, excellence in deliberation is correctness with respect to what is beneficial -- correctness in the end, the manner, and the time.

(4) Finally, it is possible to have deliberated well either in the unqualified sense or with reference to a particular end. Excellence in deliberation in the unqualified sense succeeds with reference to what is the end in the unqualified sense, while excellence in deliberation in a particular sense succeeds relative to a particular end.

If, then, it is characteristic of practically wise people to have deliberated well, excellence in deliberation will be correctness with regard to what conduces to the end of which practical wisdom is the true apprehension.
elegant: Excellence in deliberation requires four conditions: (1) it must aim at genuine goods, not merely achieve whatever one happens to want; (2) it must reach the right conclusion through correct reasoning, not by lucky accident; (3) it must arrive at the right result in the right manner and in the right time; and (4) in its fullest sense, it must aim at what is good without qualification. It is the correctness of reasoning that leads to the end that practical wisdom truly apprehends.
summary: Aristotle specifies four conditions for genuine excellence in deliberation: it must aim at true goods (not merely achieve bad goals cleverly); it must use correct reasoning (not reach good conclusions through faulty logic); it must operate in the right timeframe; and in the fullest sense, it must aim at the unconditionally good. Excellence in deliberation is the correct reasoning toward the end that practical wisdom accurately grasps.
distilled: Genuine excellence in deliberation requires reaching the right end, by the right reasoning, in the right manner and time, aimed at what is unconditionally good.
notes: The four conditions progressively narrow the definition: not just any success, not accidental success, not slow success, and ultimately success directed at the unqualified good (the end of Book I: eudaimonia). -- The case of the clever but vicious person (1) is important: cleverness (deinotes) achieves whatever one aims at, but euboulia requires that the aim itself be good. This is developed further in 6.35. -- The mention of a 'false middle term' (2) uses the language of syllogistic logic from the Prior Analytics to describe a case where one reasons fallaciously but luckily reaches a true conclusion.

refashioned:
Comprehension, and goodness of comprehension, in virtue of which people are said to be 'understanding' or 'of good understanding,' are neither entirely the same as opinion or scientific knowledge (for then everyone would possess comprehension), nor are they any one of the particular sciences such as medicine or geometry. For comprehension is not about things that are eternal and unchangeable, nor about just any subject that comes into being, but about things that may become subjects of questioning and deliberation. Hence it concerns the same objects as practical wisdom.

But comprehension and practical wisdom are not the same thing. For practical wisdom issues commands -- its end is what ought or ought not to be done -- while comprehension only judges. (Comprehension is the same as goodness of comprehension, and people of understanding are the same as people of good understanding.)

Now comprehension is neither the possession nor the acquisition of practical wisdom. Rather, just as 'learning' can mean the exercise of knowledge one already has, 'understanding' applies to the exercise of the faculty of opinion for the purpose of judging what someone else says about matters with which practical wisdom is concerned -- and judging soundly, since 'well' and 'soundly' are the same thing. This is the origin of the term 'understanding' as applied to people of 'good understanding': it comes from the application of the word to the grasping of truth, for we often call such grasping 'understanding.'
elegant: Comprehension (synesis) concerns the same domain as practical wisdom -- matters subject to deliberation -- but differs in function: practical wisdom commands action, while comprehension judges. It is the capacity to evaluate soundly what another person says about practical matters, exercising opinion for the purpose of accurate judgment rather than directing action oneself.
summary: Comprehension (synesis) shares its subject matter with practical wisdom -- both concern matters open to deliberation -- but serves a different function. While practical wisdom commands action, comprehension evaluates and judges. It is the ability to assess soundly what others say about practical matters, a capacity for accurate judgment rather than for directing one's own conduct.
distilled: Comprehension is the capacity to judge soundly about matters of deliberation -- sharing practical wisdom's domain but evaluating rather than commanding.
notes: Synesis is often translated as 'understanding' or 'comprehension.' Aristotle carefully distinguishes it from phronesis: synesis is a critical, evaluative capacity (it judges), while phronesis is a directive one (it commands). -- The distinction between commanding and judging maps roughly onto a distinction between active decision-making and the evaluative capacity of an adviser or critic. -- The etymological observation connecting synesis with the act of grasping (synienai) truth illustrates Aristotle's typical practice of drawing philosophical distinctions from ordinary language usage.

refashioned: What is called judgment -- in virtue of which people are said to 'have sympathetic judgment' and to 'be sympathetic judges' -- is the correct discernment of what is equitable. This is shown by the fact that we say the equitable person is above all others a person of sympathetic judgment, and we identify equity with sympathetic judgment about certain matters. And sympathetic judgment is judgment that discerns what is equitable and does so correctly; and correct judgment is that which judges what is true.
elegant: Judgment (gnome) is the correct discernment of what is equitable. The person of sympathetic judgment is the equitable person, who accurately perceives when strict rules should be tempered by fairness in particular circumstances.
summary: Judgment (gnome) is defined as the correct discernment of equity -- the ability to perceive when strict rules should be adjusted to fit particular circumstances fairly. The person of good judgment and the equitable person are essentially the same.
distilled: Judgment is the correct perception of what equity requires in particular cases where strict rules fall short.
notes: Gnome (judgment, fellow-feeling, sympathetic judgment) is connected to epieikeia (equity), which Aristotle discusses more fully in Book V.10, 1137a31-1138a3. Equity is a correction of law where law falls short due to its universality. -- The connection between gnome and equity makes judgment a quasi-perceptual capacity for fairness in particular cases -- closely related to phronesis but focused specifically on equitable treatment. -- The Greek gnome also means 'maxim' or 'considered opinion,' and the double sense is relevant: good judgment both grasps and can articulate what fairness requires.

refashioned:
Now all the states we have examined converge, as one would expect, on the same point. When we speak of judgment, comprehension, practical wisdom, and intuitive reason, we credit the same people with possessing judgment, having reached years of reason, and having practical wisdom and comprehension. For all these faculties deal with ultimates -- that is, with particulars. Being a person of comprehension and of good or sympathetic judgment consists in being able to judge about the matters with which practical wisdom is concerned, since equitable treatment is common to all good people in their relations with others.

Now all things that must be done fall among particulars or ultimates, for the practically wise person must not only know particular facts, but comprehension and judgment are also concerned with things to be done, and these are ultimates. And intuitive reason is concerned with ultimates in both directions: both first terms and last terms are objects of intuitive reason, not of argument. The intuitive reason presupposed by demonstrations grasps the unchangeable first principles, while the intuitive reason involved in practical reasoning grasps the last and variable fact -- the minor premise. For these variable particular facts are the starting points for apprehending the end, since universals are reached from particulars. Of these particulars, therefore, we must have perception, and this perception is intuitive reason.
elegant: Judgment, comprehension, practical wisdom, and intuitive reason all converge on the same point: the grasp of particular, concrete facts. Intuitive reason operates at both extremes -- grasping indemonstrable first principles in theory and grasping the ultimate particular facts in practice. In practical reasoning, it is through perception of particulars that we arrive at universal ends, for universals are built from particulars.
summary: All the practical intellectual faculties -- judgment, comprehension, practical wisdom, and intuitive reason -- converge on the ability to grasp particulars. Intuitive reason works at both extremes: grasping first principles in theoretical reasoning and grasping concrete particular facts in practical reasoning. Since universals are reached from particulars, this perceptual grasp of the particular situation is the foundation of all practical knowledge.
distilled: All practical intellectual virtues converge on the grasp of particulars, and intuitive reason operates at both extremes -- first principles in theory and concrete facts in practice.
notes: This passage reveals a deep structural symmetry in Aristotle's epistemology: nous (intuitive reason) is needed both at the 'top' (first principles) and the 'bottom' (particular facts), while the middle is handled by demonstrative reasoning. -- The claim that 'universals are reached from particulars' echoes the doctrine of Posterior Analytics II.19 on how nous arises from experience. -- The convergence of all practical intellectual virtues on particulars underscores Aristotle's emphasis that practical knowledge is irreducibly concrete and situation-specific, not merely a matter of applying general rules.

refashioned:
This is why these states are thought to be natural endowments. While no one is thought to be a philosopher by nature, people are thought to possess judgment, comprehension, and intuitive reason by nature. This is shown by the fact that we think these capacities correspond to our time of life, and that a particular age brings with it intuitive reason and judgment -- implying that nature is the cause.

Hence intuitive reason is both beginning and end, for demonstrations proceed from first principles and are about these same principles. Therefore we ought to attend to the undemonstrated sayings and opinions of experienced and older people -- or of people of practical wisdom -- no less than to formal demonstrations. For because experience has given them an eye, they see correctly.

We have stated, then, what practical and philosophic wisdom are, what each is concerned with, and that each is the virtue of a different part of the soul.
elegant: Judgment, comprehension, and intuitive reason are thought to be natural endowments that develop with age, unlike philosophic wisdom which must be learned. The experienced eye of older, practically wise people perceives truths that cannot be formally demonstrated -- their unproven sayings and opinions deserve no less respect than rigorous proofs.
summary: People naturally develop judgment, comprehension, and intuitive reason with age and experience, unlike philosophic wisdom which must be deliberately acquired. Because experienced people have developed a perceptual 'eye' for truth through long practice, their undemonstrated opinions deserve as much respect as formal proofs. Aristotle concludes the survey of practical and philosophic wisdom as virtues of different parts of the soul.
distilled: Experience gives older and practically wise people a perceptive eye for truth, making their undemonstrated insights as worthy of attention as formal proofs.
notes: The claim that we should attend to the 'undemonstrated sayings' (aphantos lexeis) of the experienced is a remarkable concession from the author of the Posterior Analytics, where demonstration is the gold standard of knowledge. It shows Aristotle recognizes the limits of formal reasoning in practical domains. -- The metaphor of experience giving an 'eye' (omma) echoes the broader Greek tradition connecting wisdom with vision (cf. Plato, Republic VII, the allegory of the cave). -- This passage summarizes the results of Book VI's first eleven chapters before turning to the utility objection in 6.32.

refashioned:
Difficulties might be raised about the utility of these intellectual qualities. (1) Philosophic wisdom contemplates none of the things that will make a person happy, for it is not concerned with anything coming into being. And though practical wisdom has this concern, for what purpose do we actually need it? Practical wisdom is the quality of mind concerned with things just, noble, and good for human beings, but these are precisely the things a good person does. We are no better able to act for knowing them, if the virtues are states of character -- just as we are no better able to act for knowing what is healthy and sound, if 'healthy' refers not to what produces health but to what issues from a state of health. We are no better at acting healthily just because we possess the art of medicine or gymnastics.

(2) If the point is that we should have practical wisdom not for knowing moral truths but for becoming good, then practical wisdom will be useless to those who are already good. Again, it is useless to those who lack virtue, for it makes no difference whether they possess practical wisdom themselves or follow others who have it -- and it would be enough for us to do what we do with health: we wish to be healthy but do not learn the art of medicine.

(3) Besides, it would seem strange if practical wisdom, being inferior to philosophic wisdom, is to have authority over it, as seems implied by the fact that the art which produces something rules and commands regarding that thing.
elegant: Three objections arise: (1) Philosophic wisdom produces nothing useful, and practical wisdom tells us what the good person already does -- knowing virtue does not make us more virtuous, just as knowing medicine does not make us healthier. (2) Practical wisdom is useless both to the already-good and to the bad, who could simply follow others' advice. (3) It seems absurd that practical wisdom, which is inferior, should have authority over philosophic wisdom.
summary: Aristotle raises three objections to the utility of intellectual virtue. First, philosophic wisdom produces nothing practical, and practical wisdom merely identifies what good people already do. Second, practical wisdom seems unnecessary for both the virtuous (who already act well) and the vicious (who could just follow advice). Third, it seems paradoxical that the lesser virtue (practical wisdom) should govern the greater (philosophic wisdom).
distilled: The utility of intellectual virtue faces serious objections: philosophic wisdom produces nothing practical, practical wisdom seems redundant for both good and bad people, and the hierarchy between the two appears paradoxical.
notes: These three aporia (difficulties) structure the remainder of Book VI. Aristotle's method of raising objections before resolving them is characteristic of his dialectical approach (cf. Metaphysics III for a systematic deployment of aporiai). -- Objection (1) parallels the debate about whether knowledge of the good is sufficient for being good, which Aristotle attributes to Socrates (cf. VII.2, 1145b25-27). -- Objection (3) about the hierarchy of practical and philosophic wisdom connects to the broader question of whether the contemplative or practical life is superior (Book X.7-8).

refashioned:
These, then, are the questions we must discuss. So far we have only stated the difficulties.

(1) First, let us say that these states must be worthy of choice in themselves, because they are the virtues of the two parts of the soul respectively, even if neither of them produces anything further.
elegant: Even if philosophic and practical wisdom produced nothing, they would still be worth choosing as the virtues -- the excellences -- of their respective parts of the soul.
summary: Before resolving the detailed objections, Aristotle makes a foundational point: philosophic and practical wisdom are intrinsically worth having because they are the excellences of the two rational parts of the soul, regardless of any practical output they might produce.
distilled: Intellectual virtues are worth possessing for their own sake as the excellences of the rational soul, independent of any practical results.
notes: This response draws on the intrinsic value of virtue established in Book I, where happiness is defined as activity of the soul in accordance with virtue (I.7, 1098a16-18). -- The argument that virtues are choiceworthy in themselves, independent of consequences, represents an important strand in Aristotle's ethical thinking that resists purely instrumentalist accounts of virtue. -- This is the first of three responses to the objections raised in 6.32.

refashioned:
(2) Secondly, they do in fact produce something -- not, however, as medicine produces health, but as health produces health. In this way, philosophic wisdom produces happiness: being a part of complete virtue, by being possessed and exercised it makes a person happy.

(3) Again, a person's proper function is achieved only through the combination of practical wisdom and moral virtue. Virtue makes us aim at the right end, and practical wisdom makes us take the right means. (Of the fourth part of the soul -- the nutritive -- there is no such virtue, for there is nothing it is in its power to do or not to do.)

(4) As for the objection that practical wisdom makes us no better able to do what is noble and just, let us begin a little further back. We say that some people who perform just acts are not necessarily just -- for instance, those who do what the laws require unwillingly, or through ignorance, or for some extraneous reason, and not for the sake of the acts themselves (though they do what they should and everything the good person ought to do). Similarly, it seems, to be truly good one must be in a certain state when performing these acts -- one must do them as a result of choice and for the sake of the acts themselves.
elegant: Philosophic wisdom produces happiness not as a cause produces an effect but as health produces healthy activity -- by being a part of complete virtue. Practical wisdom and moral virtue work together: virtue sets the right end, practical wisdom finds the right means. Moreover, true goodness requires not just doing the right things but doing them from the right state of character -- from choice and for their own sake.
summary: Aristotle replies that philosophic wisdom does produce happiness, but as an intrinsic constituent rather than an external cause. Practical wisdom and moral virtue cooperate: virtue determines the right end, practical wisdom determines the right means. True goodness requires acting from the right inner state -- performing good acts from choice and for their own sake, not merely doing what happens to be right.
distilled: Virtue sets the right end and practical wisdom finds the right means; together they produce genuine goodness, which requires acting from choice and for the sake of what is noble.
notes: The distinction between acting rightly and acting from the right state of character is fundamental to Aristotle's ethics. Merely doing the right thing is not sufficient for virtue; one must do it knowingly, by choice, and from a firm character (cf.
