II.4, 1105a28-33). -- The formula 'virtue makes the aim right, practical wisdom makes the means right' is one of the most cited in Aristotelian ethics and has generated debate about whether Aristotle's practical wisdom is merely instrumental. -- The claim that sophia produces eudaimonia 'as health produces health' uses the formal-cause model rather than the efficient-cause model, making sophia a constitutive part of flourishing.

refashioned:
Now virtue makes the choice right, but the question of what things should naturally be done to carry out our choice belongs not to virtue but to another faculty. We must give this matter closer attention and explain it more clearly.

There is a faculty called cleverness, which is the ability to perform the actions that tend toward whatever goal we have set and to hit that goal. If the goal is noble, this cleverness is praiseworthy; if the goal is base, it is mere cunning. Hence we call even practically wise people clever or shrewd. Practical wisdom is not this faculty of cleverness, but it does not exist without it.

This eye of the soul acquires its developed state not without the aid of virtue, as has been said and as is clear. For the practical syllogisms that deal with action involve a starting point: 'Since the end -- that is, what is best -- is of such and such a nature' (whatever it may be; let it be whatever we please for the sake of argument). This starting point is not evident except to the good person, for wickedness distorts us and causes us to be deceived about the starting points of action.

Therefore it is evident that it is impossible to be practically wise without being good.
elegant: Cleverness is the ability to achieve whatever goal one sets; it is praiseworthy when directed at noble ends, mere cunning when directed at base ones. Practical wisdom requires cleverness but is not reducible to it, because it also requires virtue: only the good person can perceive the correct starting points of action. Wickedness distorts our perception of ends. Therefore, one cannot be practically wise without being morally good.
summary: Aristotle distinguishes cleverness (the ability to achieve any goal effectively) from practical wisdom (which requires that the goal be genuinely good). Practical wisdom depends on cleverness but goes beyond it by requiring moral virtue, since only the good person correctly perceives the true ends of action. Wickedness corrupts our grasp of what is worth pursuing. Therefore, practical wisdom and moral goodness are inseparable.
distilled: Practical wisdom requires moral goodness because only the good person correctly perceives the true ends of action, which wickedness distorts.
notes: Deinotes (cleverness) is morally neutral -- it becomes practical wisdom only when combined with correct ends, i.e., with moral virtue. This distinction is Aristotle's answer to the Socratic identification of virtue with knowledge: knowledge of means (cleverness) is not virtue unless the ends are right. -- The 'eye of the soul' (omma tes psyches) echoes Plato's Republic 533d, where the dialectician's 'eye of the soul' is drawn upward. Aristotle repurposes the metaphor for practical perception. -- The conclusion -- 'it is impossible to be practically wise without being good' -- is the capstone of Book VI's argument and establishes the mutual dependence of intellectual and moral virtue.

refashioned:
We must therefore consider virtue once more, for virtue is related to practical wisdom in a way that parallels the relationship between practical wisdom and cleverness. As practical wisdom stands to cleverness -- not the same, but similar -- so natural virtue stands to virtue in the strict sense.

Everyone thinks that each type of character belongs to its possessor in some sense by nature: from birth we are disposed toward justice, self-control, courage, and the other moral qualities. But we seek something more as genuine goodness in the strict sense -- we seek for these qualities to be present in a different way. For both children and animals have natural dispositions toward these qualities, but without reason these dispositions are evidently harmful. One can see at least this much: just as a powerful body moving without sight may stumble badly because it lacks sight, so too with natural virtue without reason.

But if a person once acquires reason, that makes all the difference in action; and their state, while still resembling what it was, will then be virtue in the strict sense. Therefore, just as in the part of us that forms opinions there are two types -- cleverness and practical wisdom -- so in the moral part there are two types: natural virtue and virtue in the strict sense. And of these, virtue in the strict sense involves practical wisdom.
elegant: As practical wisdom is to cleverness, so true virtue is to natural virtue. We are born with natural tendencies toward courage, justice, and other qualities, but without reason these dispositions can be harmful -- like a powerful body moving without sight. When reason is added through practical wisdom, natural virtue becomes true virtue. There are thus two moral types: raw natural virtue and genuine virtue informed by practical wisdom.
summary: Aristotle draws a parallel: as practical wisdom relates to cleverness, so true virtue relates to natural virtue. We are born with tendencies toward various moral qualities, but without rational guidance these can be harmful. When practical wisdom informs natural dispositions, they become genuine virtues. True virtue therefore requires practical wisdom, just as practical wisdom requires moral goodness.
distilled: Natural moral dispositions become genuine virtues only when guided by practical wisdom, just as cleverness becomes practical wisdom only when directed at genuinely good ends.
notes: The distinction between natural virtue (physike arete) and virtue in the strict sense (kyria arete) is crucial to Aristotle's moral psychology. Natural virtues are inborn dispositions; full virtues require the addition of practical wisdom. -- The metaphor of a strong body without sight powerfully conveys how natural virtues without reason can lead to harmful action. -- This passage completes the circle of mutual dependence: moral virtue needs practical wisdom to become genuine, and practical wisdom needs moral virtue to perceive correct ends (6.35). -- The connection between natural virtue and habituation (ethismos, Book II) remains debated: natural virtue provides the raw material that moral education shapes.

refashioned:
This is why some say that all the virtues are forms of practical wisdom, and why Socrates was in one respect on the right track while in another he went astray. In thinking that all the virtues were forms of practical wisdom, he was wrong; but in saying they implied practical wisdom, he was right.

This is confirmed by the fact that even now, when people define virtue, they name the state of character and its objects and then add 'that state which accords with the right rule.' And the right rule is that which accords with practical wisdom. All people, then, seem somehow to intuit that this kind of state -- the kind that accords with practical wisdom -- is virtue.
elegant: Socrates was partly right: all virtues imply practical wisdom, though they are not identical with it. Even common definitions of virtue confirm this, since people always add 'in accordance with the right rule' -- and the right rule is practical wisdom.
summary: Aristotle assesses the Socratic thesis that virtue is knowledge. Socrates was wrong to identify virtues with practical wisdom, but right to say they require it. This is confirmed by how people naturally define virtue: they always include the phrase 'in accordance with the right rule,' and the right rule just is practical wisdom.
distilled: Socrates was right that virtue requires practical wisdom but wrong to reduce all virtue to it -- virtue accords with the right rule, and practical wisdom is that right rule.
notes: The Socratic doctrine that virtue is knowledge (episteme) is discussed in several Platonic dialogues, notably Protagoras 361b and Meno 87c-89a. Aristotle's correction -- virtues involve (meta) a rational principle but are not (identical with) rational principles -- is a characteristically nuanced revision. -- The 'right rule' (orthos logos) here receives its definitive identification with phronesis, completing the promise made in 6.1. -- This passage is important for understanding Aristotle's relationship to his teacher Plato and to the Socratic tradition more broadly.

refashioned:
But we must go a little further. For it is not merely the state that accords with the right rule that is virtue, but the state that implies the presence of the right rule. And practical wisdom is the right rule about such matters.

Socrates thought the virtues were rules or rational principles -- since he thought they were all forms of scientific knowledge -- while we hold that they involve a rational principle.
elegant: True virtue is not merely in accordance with the right rule but embodies it -- the right rule must be present within the virtuous state itself. Practical wisdom is that right rule. Socrates identified virtues with rational principles; Aristotle holds that they involve rational principles.
summary: Aristotle sharpens the distinction: virtue is not just acting in line with the right rule externally, but having the right rule -- practical wisdom -- as an internal component. Socrates treated virtues as identical to rational principles (forms of knowledge); Aristotle insists they involve rational principles without being reducible to them.
distilled: Virtue does not merely follow the right rule from outside but contains practical wisdom as an internal, constitutive element.
notes: The shift from 'in accordance with' (kata) to 'involving the presence of' (meta) the right rule is a subtle but important philosophical distinction. Merely acting in accordance with a rule could be accidental or externally motivated; having the rule present within oneself implies understanding and endorsement. -- This refines the definition of virtue from Book II.6, 1106b36-1107a2. -- The correction of Socrates is diplomatically expressed but philosophically significant: Aristotle preserves the intellectual element in virtue while insisting on its integration with character and desire.

refashioned:
It is clear, then, from what has been said, that it is not possible to be good in the strict sense without practical wisdom, nor practically wise without moral virtue.

In this way, we can also refute the dialectical argument that the virtues exist in separation from each other. The objection would be: the same person is not best equipped by nature for all the virtues, so they might have acquired one virtue without yet having acquired another. This is possible with respect to natural virtues, but not with respect to the virtues that qualify someone as unconditionally good. For with the presence of the one quality, practical wisdom, all the virtues will be present.

It is also plain that even if practical wisdom had no practical value, we would still need it because it is the virtue of its part of the soul. And it is plain too that choice will not be right without practical wisdom any more than without virtue, for the one determines the end while the other makes us do the things that lead to the end.
elegant: One cannot be truly good without practical wisdom, nor practically wise without moral virtue. This unity thesis also refutes the claim that virtues can exist independently: natural virtues may be separate, but genuine virtues come as a package with practical wisdom. Virtue determines the right end; practical wisdom determines the right means. Neither suffices without the other.
summary: Aristotle establishes the reciprocal dependence of practical wisdom and moral virtue, and uses this to argue for the unity of the virtues. While natural virtues can exist independently, genuine virtues cannot -- possessing practical wisdom entails possessing all the virtues. Virtue sets the correct end and practical wisdom identifies the correct means; right choice requires both.
distilled: Genuine virtue and practical wisdom are mutually dependent, and possessing practical wisdom guarantees the unity of all the virtues.
notes: The unity of the virtues (hen echonti panti) is one of the most debated theses in Aristotelian ethics. Strong unity holds that you cannot have any one virtue without having them all; weak unity holds merely that the virtues are deeply interconnected. This passage supports the strong version for kyria arete (virtue in the strict sense). -- The argument structure is: practical wisdom requires moral virtue (6.35); moral virtue requires practical wisdom (6.36); therefore they come together; and since practical wisdom is one, it unifies all the virtues. -- The final formula -- 'virtue determines the end, practical wisdom the means' -- has become perhaps the most influential summary of Aristotle's practical philosophy.

refashioned:
But practical wisdom is not supreme over philosophic wisdom -- that is, over the superior part of us -- any more than the art of medicine is supreme over health. Medicine does not use health but provides for its coming into being; it issues orders for the sake of health, not to health itself.

Furthermore, claiming that practical wisdom rules philosophic wisdom would be like saying that the art of politics rules the gods because it issues orders about all the affairs of the state.
elegant: Practical wisdom does not rule over philosophic wisdom, just as medicine does not rule health but serves it. Practical wisdom provides the conditions for philosophic wisdom to flourish, issuing orders for its sake, not to it. Claiming otherwise would be like saying politics rules the gods merely because it governs public affairs.
summary: Aristotle resolves the final objection by clarifying the relationship between practical and philosophic wisdom. Practical wisdom does not command philosophic wisdom but serves it -- as medicine serves health by creating conditions for it, not by directing it. The analogy of politics 'ruling the gods' shows the absurdity of inverting this hierarchy.
distilled: Practical wisdom serves philosophic wisdom by creating the conditions for its exercise, just as medicine serves health without claiming authority over it.
notes: This passage resolves objection (3) from 6.32 about the paradox of practical wisdom appearing to rule over philosophic wisdom. -- The medicine-health analogy is crucial: practical wisdom arranges the conditions of life so that philosophical contemplation can occur, but does not dictate the content of contemplation. -- The politics-gods analogy is witty and memorable: politics may organize temple festivals, but it would be absurd to claim it therefore 'rules' the gods. -- This sets up the argument of Book X.7-8, where Aristotle will argue that contemplation (theoria) is the highest form of happiness, with practical life as secondary. -- The final position of this passage in Book VI gives it programmatic significance: intellectual virtue serves both practical and contemplative ends, with the contemplative being the higher.
